hook: A warren of 17th-century streets and houses buried beneath the City Chambers, sealed up and walked through 400 years on.
facts: Named after Mary King, a merchant burgess who lived and traded on the close in the 17th century.
Partially demolished and buried when the Royal Exchange (now the City Chambers) was built on top of it from 1753.
The last resident, Andrew Chesney, left in 1902 after a compulsory purchase order for GBP 400.
Re-opened to the public in 2003 as a guided visitor attraction, now operated by Continuum Attractions.
Lies directly under the City Chambers building on the Royal Mile in the Old Town.
legend: The close has had a reputation for hauntings since at least the 17th century; visitors leave gifts at the shrine of 'Annie', a girl said to haunt one of the rooms.
Popular tradition holds that plague victims were walled up alive inside the close, though historians dispute this.

sources.note: Wikipedia infobox gives coordinates 55.95028 N, -3.19028 W; history, dates, and Annie shrine.
Official attraction site corroborating location under the City Chambers and the 2003 opening.

w: A warren of 17th-century streets and tenements, sealed up and buried when the Royal Exchange rose over them from 1753. The last resident held out until 1902. Now you walk the buried close 400 years on, past the shrine where visitors leave gifts for 'Annie,' said to haunt one room. The plague victims walled up alive? Just a tale.

hook: Scotland's oldest museum collection, home to a pocketbook bound in the skin of murderer William Burke.
facts: Housed in a Playfair-designed building on Nicolson Street, completed in 1832 for the Royal College of Surgeons of Edinburgh.
Displays the death mask and a pocketbook made from the tanned skin of William Burke, executed 28 January 1829.
Burke's body was publicly dissected by anatomist Alexander Monro tertius after his hanging.
Holds Charles Bell's oil paintings of gunshot wounds from the Battle of Corunna (1809) and the Greig skull collection.
Includes a recreated 19th-century dentist's surgery in the Menzies Campbell dental collection.

sources.note: Wikipedia gives coordinates 55.9467 N, -3.1853 W; architect Playfair, 1832; Burke artefacts and collections.
Official Surgeons' Hall Museums site corroborating the Burke pocketbook and collections.

w: Scotland's oldest museum collection, behind a Playfair facade of 1832 — and keeper of a pocketbook bound in the tanned skin of William Burke, the murderer hanged in 1829 and then publicly dissected. His death mask is here too, beside Charles Bell's unflinching oils of Napoleonic gunshot wounds. Not for the faint of stomach.

hook: A labyrinth of hand-carved sandstone chambers under a suburban street whose origin and purpose nobody can explain.
facts: A series of underground passages and chambers carved from sandstone beneath Gilmerton, a former mining village in south-east Edinburgh.
First documented in 1721, when blacksmith George Paterson was accused of selling alcohol on the Sabbath to visitors of his 'caves'.
Extensive archaeological research has failed to establish who dug it, when, or why.
2017 ground-penetrating radar work suggested the network extends beyond the currently exposed passages.
Restored over five years and opened as a visitor attraction in 2003.
legend: Theories of use include a Covenanters' refuge, a smugglers' hideout, a drinking den for local gentry, and a meeting place for a Hellfire Club.

sources.note: Wikipedia gives coordinates 55.90556 N, -3.13333 W; George Paterson 1721, 2003 opening, theories of use.
Undiscovered Scotland corroborating location, mystery, and restoration.

w: Hand-carved sandstone tunnels beneath a southern suburb, and nobody can say who dug them, when, or why. First recorded in 1721, when a blacksmith was hauled up for serving drink in his 'caves' on the Sabbath. Covenanters' refuge, smugglers' den, Hellfire Club — every theory has takers, none has proof. Edinburgh's deepest shrug.

hook: A 1622 tower-house up a Royal Mile close devoted to Burns, Scott and Stevenson, with their desks, presses and personal relics.
facts: Set in Lady Stair's House, built in 1622 for Sir William Gray of Pittendrum, in Lady Stair's Close off the Lawnmarket.
Celebrates three Scottish writers: Robert Burns, Walter Scott and Robert Louis Stevenson.
Holds Burns's writing desk and the Ballantyne hand-press used to print Scott's Waverley novels.
Displays Robert Louis Stevenson's riding boots and a ring given to him by a Samoan chief.
Surrounded by Makars' Court, whose flagstones are inscribed with quotations from Scottish writers.

sources.note: Wikipedia gives coordinates 55.9497 N, -3.1937 W; three writers and held objects.
City of Edinburgh Museums official page corroborating Lady Stair's House and collections.

w: Three giants of Scottish letters — Burns, Scott and Stevenson — gathered in a turreted tower-house of 1622 up a Lawnmarket close. Burns's writing desk is here, the Ballantyne press that printed the Waverley novels, and Stevenson's riding boots beside a ring from a Samoan chief. The courtyard flagstones outside are carved with their words.

hook: The domed tomb of 'Bloody Mackenzie' beside the Covenanters' Prison, said to be the most haunted spot in the world.
facts: Italianate domed mausoleum in Greyfriars Kirkyard designed by architect James Smith, modelled on Bramante's Tempietto di San Pietro.
Final resting place of Sir George Mackenzie of Rosehaugh (1636/8-1691), Lord Advocate, nicknamed 'Bloody Mackenzie'.
Adjoins the Covenanters' Prison, where some 1,200 prisoners were held after the 1679 Battle of Bothwell Bridge.
Reports of a violent 'Mackenzie Poltergeist' began in 1999, after a rough sleeper disturbed the tomb.
The Covenanters' Prison is kept locked and accessible only on guided tours.
legend: Visitors to the locked prison report unexplained bruises, scratches and cold spots blamed on the Mackenzie Poltergeist; several have reportedly collapsed inside.

sources.note: Wikipedia gives kirkyard coordinates 55.94667 N, -3.19222 W; mausoleum architect James Smith, Mackenzie 1691, Covenanters' Prison 1679.
Atlas Obscura corroborating the mausoleum, Mackenzie and the poltergeist reports.

w: The domed tomb of 'Bloody Mackenzie,' the Lord Advocate who hounded the Covenanters to their graves, now keeping company with them in Greyfriars. Beside it sits the locked Covenanters' Prison; since a rough sleeper broke in in 1999, visitors have blamed their bruises and scratches on the 'Mackenzie Poltergeist.'

hook: A tiny Cowgate chapel holding the only intact pre-Reformation stained glass left in Scotland.
facts: Built 1541-1544 at 41 Cowgate, funded by merchant Michael MacQueen (d. 1537) and his widow Jonet Rynd.
Contains the only intact pre-Reformation stained glass window surviving in situ in Scotland, including the Royal Arms of Scotland and arms of Mary of Guise.
Hosted a General Assembly of the Church of Scotland in April 1578.
In 1689 the heads and hands of martyred Covenanters were gathered here before burial in Greyfriars.
A Category A listed building, it served as the hall of the Incorporated Trades of Edinburgh from 1596.

sources.note: Wikipedia gives coordinates 55.94796 N, -3.19245 W; 1541-44 build, MacQueen/Rynd, stained glass, Covenanter remains 1689.
Scottish Reformation Society (current occupant) corroborating the stained glass and history.

w: A tiny chapel down the Cowgate, built in the 1540s by a merchant and his widow — and holding the only intact pre-Reformation stained glass left in place in Scotland. A General Assembly met here in 1578; in 1689 the heads and hands of martyred Covenanters were gathered here before burial. Small room, long memory.

hook: A hidden 17th-century-style parterre garden tucked down a narrow close off the Royal Mile.
facts: Entered through Dunbar's Close at 137 Canongate, beside the Canongate Kirk, running down towards Calton Road.
Laid out in the style of the 17th-century town-house gardens that once lined the rear of Royal Mile properties.
Designed in 1978-79 by landscape architect Seamus Filor for the Mushroom Trust.
Gifted to the City of Edinburgh by the Mushroom Trust as a public garden.
Divided into formal sections of clipped hedges, parterres and trees, planted with species popular in the 17th century.

sources.note: Parks & Gardens gives OS National Grid Reference NT 26488 73815 (BNG 326488, 673815), which converts to approx. 55.9521 N, -3.1804 W; design history and Mushroom Trust.
Atlas Obscura corroborating location at 137 Canongate and the hidden 17th-century-style garden.

w: Step off the Royal Mile through a narrow Canongate close and a formal 17th-century garden opens up — clipped hedges, parterres and trees, all planted to the taste of three centuries ago. Seamus Filor laid it out in 1978, in the style of the long-lost town-house gardens that once ran behind the Mile. A green secret, hidden in plain sight.
